coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and flame inhibition efficiency of a novel Fe-based metal complex with phosphorus-containing structure
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Haoran
Hu, Yong
Jiang, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Owing to the increased depletion risk and cost of phosphate mines, the demand for low phosphorus or ecological phosphorus flame inhibitor is increasing. This study reports an experimental exploration of the combustion inhibition efficiency of bio-based transition metal complex materials containing iron and phosphorus, namely, iron @ phosphorus complex (Fe-PMC). The aim is to develop a new type of flame inhibitor with an iron-phosphorus interaction effect to replace pure phosphorus extinguishing agents. The synthesis of Fe-PMC was characterized by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-Ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Scanning electron microscope. The Fe-PMC (ⅰ) is the mesh porous structure connected by the accumulation of small particles, (ⅱ) is an amorphous structure with many binding sites exposed and excellent interface compatibility, (ⅲ) contains no chloride ions. Suppression tests and thermogravimetric measurements indicate that (ⅰ) Fe-PMC has a higher combustion inhibition ability than MIL-53 containing only iron, especially at low concentrations, (ⅱ) Fe-PMC reduces the laminar flame velocity of cellulose, (ⅲ) Fe-PMC greatly reduces flame temperature. The activation energy (E), pre-exponential factor (A) and char yield (Y) of cellulose pyrolysis were determined by kinetic analysis. It concluded that MIL-53 and Fe-PMC produce combustion inhibition mainly in the solid phase. These findings will contribute to the development of a flame inhibitor with low or no phosphorus and better performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal complex
Phytic acid
Iron complex
Flame inhibition
Fire-extinguishing agent

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Number of fires, injuries, deaths and economic losses in China from 2016 to 2020.
Illustration of preparation of MIL-53 and Fe-PMC.
Appearance of synthesized (a) MIL-53 (reddish brown crystals), and (b) Fe-PMC (light green powders).
SEM images of (a–c) MIL-53, and (d–f) Fe-PMC.
(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and (b) pore-size distribution curves of samples.
FT-IR spectra of the MIL-53, and Fe-PMC.
XRD patterns of the MIL-53, and Fe-PMC.
XPS survey spectra of MIL-53 and Fe-PMC.
XPS survey spectra of (a) C1s, (b) Fe2p, (c) P2p for MIL-53 and (d) C1s, (e) Fe2p, (f) P2p for Fe-PMC.
Elements content of MIL-53 and Fe-PMC (obtained by XPS semi-quantitative analysis). Error bars represent confidence interval of ±10%.
Experimental procedures for preparing paper samples with adsorbed MIL-53 or Fe-PMC.
Schematic of the experimental apparatus for the suppression trials.
Suppressing process images: (a) Pure cellulose, (b) Cellulose with MIL-53, (c) Cellulose with Fe-PMC, (d) Cellulose with ADP.
The original RGB image and corresponding binary image of flame shape: (a) Pure cellulose, (b) Cellulose with MIL-53, (c) Cellulose with Fe-PMC.
Flame temperature curves of (a) Pure cellulose, (b) Cellulose with MIL-53, (c) Cellulose with Fe-PMC.
Normalized downward flame spreading rates of MIL-53 and Fe-PMC.

TG and DTG curves of (a) pure cellulose, (b) MIL-53(4.19 ​× ​10-4 ​mol ​g −1)/cellulose, (c) MIL-53(12.68 ​× ​10-4 ​mol ​g −1)/cellulose, (d) Fe-PMC(4.32 ​× ​10-4 ​mol ​g −1)/cellulose, and (e) Fe-PMC(13.69 ​× ​10-4 ​mol ​g −1)/cellulose.
The expected stoichiometry of the targeted compounds. The data obtained by XPS semi-quantitative analysis are in parentheses.
MIL-53 and Fe-PMC concentrations in the TG analysis samples.
Apparent activation energies, pre-exponential factors, and char yields of all specimen.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and flame inhibition efficiency of a novel Fe-based metal complex with phosphorus-containing structure

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fire Science, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui, 230027, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fire Science
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fire Science, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui, 230027, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Fire Science, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui, 230027, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fire Science
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to the increased depletion risk and cost of phosphate mines, the demand for low phosphorus or ecological phosphorus flame inhibitor is increasing. This study reports an experimental exploration of the combustion inhibition efficiency of bio-based transition metal complex materials containing iron and phosphorus, namely, iron @ phosphorus complex (Fe-PMC). The aim is to develop a new type of flame inhibitor with an iron-phosphorus interaction effect to replace pure phosphorus extinguishing agents. The synthesis of Fe-PMC was characterized by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-Ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Scanning electron microscope. The Fe-PMC (ⅰ) is the mesh porous structure connected by the accumulation of small particles, (ⅱ) is an amorphous structure with many binding sites exposed and excellent interface compatibility, (ⅲ) contains no chloride ions. Suppression tests and thermogravimetric measurements indicate that (ⅰ) Fe-PMC has a higher combustion inhibition ability than MIL-53 containing only iron, especially at low concentrations, (ⅱ) Fe-PMC reduces the laminar flame velocity of cellulose, (ⅲ) Fe-PMC greatly reduces flame temperature. The activation energy (E), pre-exponential factor (A) and char yield (Y) of cellulose pyrolysis were determined by kinetic analysis. It concluded that MIL-53 and Fe-PMC produce combustion inhibition mainly in the solid phase. These findings will contribute to the development of a flame inhibitor with low or no phosphorus and better performance.

Mil-53 (iron) and Fe-PMC (iron, phosphorus) were synthesized by solvothermal process, and they were uniformly adsorbed on rectangular cellulose beds. The flame suppression efficiency of cellulose beds loaded with different amounts of Mil-53 and Fe-PMC was evaluated by down fire spreading rate and flame morphology.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: According to the China Fire Database, more than 252000 fires occurred in 2020, causing 1183 injuries, 775 deaths and 4 billion yuan of economic losses, respectively [1] (Fig. 1 ). Proper use of fire extinguishing agents can effectively reduce fire hazards [2]. At present, the commonly used ABC dry powder extinguishing agent is mainly ammonium dihydrogen phosphate, and other commercial dry powder extinguishing agent mostly contains phosphorus [3]. However, in recent years, the high quality of phosphate resources gradually tends to be exhausted, there are reports that phosphate resources will be exhausted in the early next century base on the current rate of consumption [4]. In addition, the price of phosphate rock rises year by year, which directly affects the production cost of phosphorus-containing fire extinguishing agents [5]. In this case, it is necessary to develop a new flame inhibitor with high fire extinguishing efficiency and little or no phosphorus.

Numerous studies have shown that the metal elements, especially cobalt, iron, nickel and copper, have better flame retardant performance than phosphorus, and have a good inhibitory effect on the formation of smoke particles and toxic gas in the combustion process. Recently, several reports of fire extinguishing materials containing metal elements have been found in the literature [6]. For instance, Koshiba et al. [7] explored the effects of calcium compounds such as calcium acetylacetonate, calcium acetate and sodium chloride on the downward flame spread of solid cellulose fuel, and calcium compounds showed better flame inhibition performance than ammonium dihydrogen phosphate in the experiment. Ni et al. [8] prepared zeolite powder loaded with manganese compound and used it to extinguish methane/air co-flowing flame. Experimental results show that flame suppression ability is positively correlated with the concentration of manganese compound loaded on zeolite powder. In addition to several metal compounds, such as manganese and calcium, which can scavenge free radicals in chain reactions in flames, iron has recently received much attention as a flame inhibitor material [9]. Several studies on the flame suppression ability of metal compounds have shown that iron compounds are good inhibitors. Linteris et al. [10] experimentally measured the combustion rate of methane/air premixed flame containing ferrocene and proved that ferrocene has a high flame suppression ability. Koshiba et al. [11] synthesized two iron-containing flame inhibitors, iron perfluorocarboxylato complexes and iron trifluoroacetate, and proved their flame inhibition effect in the gas phase.

Metal complex materials (MC) are common firefighting ingredients. Some MC are porous crystal materials of metal center atoms connected by organic ligands, with large specific surface area, high thermal stability, special structure and holes [12], and good compatibility with other materials [13]. The structure of MC material determines its rich design ability. The metal central atom of MC can provide metal flame retardant elements, and ligands (such as imidazoles, carboxylic acids, phosphonic acids, etc.) can provide flame-retardant elements and groups [14]. In addition, nano-mesh materials help to improve the flame suppression ability of fire extinguishing agents [15]. Hou et al. [16] synthesized nickel-based MC with flower-like morphology and graphene oxide/nickel-MC hybrid with vertical orientation channels, which had the two-dimensional structure of graphite oxide and the porosity of MC, providing the possibility to fill in flame retardant elements. Meng et al. [17] considered the synergistic flame retardancy effect and its influence on mechanical properties, selected the phosphazene derivative poly (cyclotriphosphazene-dihydroxy diphenyl sulfone) and ZIF-8 to prepare hybrid materials to meet the requirements of flame retardancy efficiency and mechanical properties. Hu et al. [18] selected iron elements as metal clusters and terephthalic acid as an organic ligand to prepare an organic-inorganic hybrid material containing iron. The experiment showed that iron elements played a crucial role in improving flame retardant performance.

In the experiment of flame retardant properties of materials containing iron element shows excellent performance [19]. And it's well known in chemistry that the common organic ligands that bind iron are carboxylate [20]. However, most carboxylate compounds do not contain flame retardant elements. Therefore, when iron ions form a complex with carboxylate without flame retardant elements, they may not have excellent flame retardant properties. To overcome the above limitations and meet the requirements of green synthesis and non-toxicity, we used cost-effective and readily available phospho-containing biomass-derived materials. Phytic acid is a biomass-derived organic phosphoric acid with the ability to chelate metal ions, and its high phosphorus content has attracted much attention [21]. It can not only form a complex with iron ions but also introduce phosphorus into the metal complex. Phytic acid is an organic acid that can complex iron that has not been previously reported in fire-related research.

The main purpose of this study is to synthesize an MC material containing both metal elements and phosphorus elements and to verify that the introduction of phosphorus can improve the flame retardant performance of iron complex. Section 2 explains the synthesis method of the metal complex and the analytical method used for characterization. For comparison, two MC containing iron and both iron and phosphorus were synthesized, referred to as MIL-53 and Fe-PMC respectively. Section 3 describes the combustion inhibition efficiency of the prepared MIL-53 and Fe-PMC according to the experiment. In section 4, the inhibition mechanism of synthesized materials is derived by using thermogravimetric data. Section 5 concludes the study.

The reagents used in this study were purchased from commercial channels without further processing and purification. Iron (Ⅲ) nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O) and p-Phthalic acid (PTA) with purities of >99% were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. Phytic acid (PA) solution with purities of 70%, Ammonium dihydrogen phosphate (ADP) and Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were offered by Sinopharm Shanghai Co., Ltd. The water used in the experiment was deionized water produced in our laboratory.

The MC used in this experiment were all prepared by the solvothermal reaction. The preparation process is shown in Fig. 2 . 0.54 ​mol/L of PTA solution was prepared, and then 2.5 ​mmol of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O was added to 10 ​mL of PTA solution. After magnetic stirring for half an hour, the uniformly dispersed mixture was transferred to a 100 ​mL autoclave. After the solution was treated at 200 ​°C for 4 ​h, it was filtered and washed with water to obtain brown powder (MIL-53), which was placed in a vacuum oven at 80 ​°C to dry for later use.

0.005 ​mmol/L of PA solution was prepared, and then 2.5 ​mmol of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O was added to 10 ​mL of PA solution. After magnetic stirring for half an hour, the pH of the uniformly dispersed mixture was adjusted to 7–8 by adding 0.1 ​mol/L NaOH aqueous solution, then it was transferred to an 80 ​mL autoclave after the solution was treated at 200 ​°C for 6 ​h, it was filtered and washed with water to obtain a white powder (Fe-PMC), which was dried in a vacuum oven at 80 ​°C for future use.

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR): The functional groups and chemical structure of as-prepared samples were determined by FT-IR. The FT-IR spectra of KBr chips containing samples were characterized by a Nicolet 8700 Fourier transform spectrometer over the wavenumber range 450–4000 ​cm−1 with a spectral resolution of 0.2 ​cm−1.

X-ray diffraction (XRD): The crystalline nature of as-prepared samples was checked by XRD. The diffraction pattern was recorded with a Smartlab 9 ​kW advance powder X-ray Cu Ka radiation spectra (Japan Science SmartLab 9 ​kW). The wavelength, voltage and current were 0.15406 ​nm, 40 ​kV and 40 ​mA, respectively.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS): The surface elements of the samples were measured using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha+). The X-ray source was Al Kα micro focusing monochromatic, the beam spot was continuously adjustable from 30 to 400 ​μm, and the step length was 5 ​μm. The vacuum degree in the analysis room was less than 2 ​× ​10-7 ​Mbar.

The Gemini450 scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to scan the micromorphology of the sample surface. The completely dried sample powders were evenly dispersed on the surface of the conductive adhesive and sprayed with gold for 100 ​s to obtain a clear image.

The automatic specific surface and porosity analyzer was used to perform nitrogen desorption tests on the specific surface area, pore volume and pore size distribution of the sample and calculate the specific surface area and pore size distribution.

As shown in Fig. 3 . The MIL-53 product and Fe-PMC product were obtained as reddish-brown crystals and light green powders, respectively. After being ground in an agate mortar, the light green powder has a finer texture and uniform particles compared with the red-brown crystals, while the red-brown crystals have different particle sizes and uneven particle size distribution.

The morphology of these samples was further studied using SEM images. It can be seen from Fig. 4 (a–c) that MIL-53 exhibits a micro-scale polyhedral structure with a diameter of 1∼10 ​μm, which is consistent with the previously reported observation results of MIL-53(Fe) [22].

Fig. 4(d–f) is the SEM image of Fe-PMC. It can be seen that the prepared material is a networked porous material with small particles stacked and connected. The size of the particles is fairly uniform with a diameter of 1∼2 ​μm. The pore network structure grows tightly on the surface of the polyhedral Fe-PMC, and this direct contact facilitates the transport of electrons and holes in the heterostructure.

N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms are shown in Fig. 5 . The specific surface area of MIL-53 and Fe-PMC was 26.97 ​m2/g and 4.12 ​m2/g, respectively. From the data of the specific surface area, it can be seen that although the templates are not used, certain pores are formed in both materials. The specific surface area of Fe-PMC is smaller than that of MIL-53, which may be because excessive phosphoric groups cover the skeleton surface of materials and are distributed in large voids [17]. The pore sizes of MIL-53 and Fe-PMC are 7.54 ​nm and 7.14 ​nm, respectively, and the micropores with diameters less than 2 ​nm and large pores with diameters greater than 50 ​nm are significantly reduced. This may be because the phosphonate groups block the small micropores and distribute them in the large pores, which are divided into smaller mesopores.

As shown in Fig. 6 , the functional group and chemical structure of Fe-PMC were studied by FT-IR spectroscopy and the obtained spectrum was compared with that of MIL-53. In FT-IR spectra of MIL-53, typical peaks are observed at 1689, 1575, 1509, 1424, 1137, 781 and 566 ​cm-1, respectively [23]. The research results of parent MIL-53 are in good agreement with published literature reports. The absorption peaks at 1689 ​cm-1 and 1575 ​cm-1 belong to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the -COO- group, and the peaks at 1509 ​cm-1 and 1424 ​cm-1 are considered to be symmetrical stretching bands of the -COO- group. These peaks confirm that the -COO- groups are in harmony with the central iron atom [24]. In addition, the peak at 945 ​cm-1 is ascribed to the -OH group bridging the iron atoms [25]. The adsorption bands between 735 and 1137 ​cm-1 are related to the stretching vibration of C-H, indicating that there is a benzene ring in the structure of MIL-53 [26]. The synthesized Fe-PMC material has absorption bands at 1022 and 1061 ​cm-1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of PO3 2− and the stretching vibration of P-O-Fe [27]. The three absorption bands are all characteristic absorption bands of the phosphoric acid group. In addition, the P-O stretching vibration of the phosphoric acid group and the C-O stretching vibration absorption band of the benzene ring appear very weak at 1250 and 1300 ​cm-1, which is due to the absorption bands of PO3 2− and P-O-Fe [28].

XRD patterns of MIL-53 and Fe-PMC in the range of 10–80° are illustrated in Fig. 7 . It clearly shows the crystalline nature of as-prepared samples. The PDF data in Jade are used to semi-qualitatively analyze the crystal properties of the samples. MIL-53 has part of the characteristic peak of Fe2O3 (PDF#33–0664) according to well-matched indexed planes including (0 1 2), (1 0 4), (1 1 0) and (1 1 3). It reveals the presence of iron in the sample, and it exists in the form of iron oxide [29]. The XRD pattern of Fe-PMC shows obvious characteristic peaks near 20°, which is consistent with the MC synthesized with phytic acid as a ligand reported in the literature [17]. The diffraction pattern for the prepared Fe-PMC has five strong peaks at 17.73°, 19.05°, 20.26°, 24.56°, and 31.83°, corresponding to (0 0 1), (1 0–1), (1 -1 -1), (3 0 1) and (3 2 1) of C4H6O6 (PDF#21–0920), respectively. It indicates that the structure of carboxylic acid exists in the iron complex synthesized by phytic acid as an organic ligand with metal iron ions.

The chemical composition of prepared MIL-53 and Fe-PMC samples was systematically studied by XPS. Fig. 8 shows the surface elements of two samples. The high binding energy signal in 532.8 ​eV found in XPS curves can be attributed to O 1s. Several low binding energy signals can be observed from the spectral signals, which represent the binding energy of Fe 2p, C 1s and P 2p, respectively. As illustrated in the spectrum of Fe-PMC, there is a Fe 2p peak and a P 2p peak at 712.64 ​eV and 133.51 ​eV, respectively, corresponding to the binding energy of standard iron and standard phosphorus. However, the characteristic peak of phosphorus was not found in the spectrum of MIL-53. It indicates that prepared Fe-PMC contains both iron and phosphorus.

The XPS measurement fine spectra of C 1s, Fe 2p and P 2p of prepared samples are shown in Fig. 9 . No peak is found in the binding energy range where phosphorus may be peaked in Fig. 9(c). The Avantage is used to perform peak fitting and element semi-quantitative analysis on the spectra of the samples. The element composition and content of the samples are shown in Fig. 10 , which illustrates that MIL-53 contains 56.44% of C, 35.24% of O and 7.33% of Fe, and Fe-PMC contains 19.07% of C, 61.06% of O, 6.24% of Fe and 13.64% of P, respectively. It means Fe-PMC materials containing both iron and phosphorus have been successfully synthesized.

Table 1 shows the expected stoichiometry of MIL-53 and Fe-PMC. In parentheses are stoichiometry obtained by XPS data fitting analysis. Due to the defects and limitations of the XPS experiment, the expected stoichiometry and XPS data are different, mainly for iron and phosphorus, but the relative ratio of each element is of some reference value. XPS data and expected stoichiometry showed the presence of phosphorus and iron in Fe-PMC.

The uniformly dispersed inhibitor was adsorbed on the thermally thin filter paper, and the combustion suppression efficiency was evaluated by measuring the downward flame spread rate (DFSR). Cellulose is a macromolecular polysaccharide composed of glucose and is flammable. The cellulose combustion method is a recognized technique for measuring the efficiency of combustion suppression. Izumikawa et al. studied the inhibitory effects of chemical flame retardants and thermal flame retardants on the burning limit of paper [30]. McCarter used a small-scale qualitative test to determine the flame retardant and inhibitory effects of 185 inorganic powders on cellulose [31]. Koshiba et al. used the experiment by measuring the downward flame spread rates of cellulose paper to characterize the flame retardant properties of two short-chain perfluoro carboxylic irons and analyze the mechanism [11]. Studies have shown that when the surface decomposition of high-temperature fuel is restricted if the flame retardant does not affect the pyrolysis or degradation process of the solid fuel, the suppression efficiency does not significantly differ between the flame retardant discharged to the burning fuel and the flame retardant adsorbed on the solid fuel [32].

Data from related studies show that the upward flame propagation rate of solid fuels (including thermally thin materials) is usually unstable compared to downward fire propagation [33]. It is because buoyant convection and diffusion gas transmission occur together in the process of upward flame propagation [34]. However, these effects can be ignored when the downward fire spreads, and the results are repeatable. In actual solid fuel combustion, upward flame propagation needs to be considered [35], but in this study, the main purpose is to explore the suppression efficiency of flame retardants, so the DFSR is measured and calculated to obtain reproducible results.

The downward flame spread of thermally thin fuel is usually calculated by Eq (1) [36]:
(1)
U =
2
λ
ρ s
c p
δ
(
T f
−
T v
T v
−
T ∞
)
,
where
U
is the DFSR,
λ
is the thermal conductivity in the gas phase,
ρ s
is the Solid fuel density,
c p
is the heat capacity of solid fuel,
δ
is the solid fuel thickness,
T f
is the flame temperature,
T v
is the evaporation temperature,
T ∞
is the ambient temperature. All variables except
T f
can be regarded as constants because the inhibitor was adsorbed on the filter paper at low concentrations. It can be seen from Eq (1) that for the downward flame spread rate of cellulose fuel, when
δ
<15 ​mm, the flame spread rate is negatively correlated with the reciprocal of the thickness, i.e.,
U ∼
δ
− 1
; In contrast, in the case of
δ
>15 ​mm, this relationship does not hold because of the thermal thickness of the sample, which leads to unstable flame spread. The research of Drysdale [37] showed that the shape and width of the thermally thin solid fuel affect the upward flame spread rate, that is, the flame spread rate ​∼ ​width1/2, and the width has little effect on the downward flame spread rate. The cellulose paper sample used in this study is sufficiently thin under thermal conditions, so its downward flame propagation is stable. The flame spread rate is also affected by factors such as ambient gas composition, ambient gas concentration, gas flow rate, ambient pressure and gravity [38]. In addition, for thermally thin fuels, the air velocity has little effect on the flame spread rate [39]. Therefore, the downward fire spread of the filter paper can directly compare the flame retardant efficiency of the flame retardant chemically.

MIL-53 and Fe-PMC were used as prepared. The prepared inhibitors were dissolved in absolute alcohol (>99%). Hard filter papers (>99% cellulose) with uniform thickness of 0.12 ​mm, density of 96 ​g ​m-2 and ash content of 0.025% were obtained from Hangzhou Xinhua Paper Co. Ltd. (China).

Fig. 11 demonstrates the experimental procedures for preparing paper samples with adsorbed prepared inhibitors. The design of these procedures referred to Koshiba's work [40] and made some improvements. First, the round filter paper was cut into a long strip of 140 ​mm long and 10 ​mm wide. The strip of paper was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 105 ​°C for 24 ​h, then weighed and recorded as
W 0
. The dried long strips of paper are immersed in an ethanol solution containing inhibitors, and the immersion time is controlled to achieve the purpose of different adsorption concentrations of different samples. The paper sheet with adsorbed inhibitor was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 105 ​°C for 24 ​h, and then weighed and recorded as
W s
. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) observation confirmed that the inhibitor particles were not agglomerated or compacted on the paper sheet, indicating that the inhibitor was uniformly adsorbed on the filter paper sample. The formula for calculating the concentration of inhibitor adsorbed per unit weight of the strip of the paper sample (
C k
) is
(2)
C k
=
(
W s
−
W 0
M
)
/
W 0
,
where
M
is the molar mass of the inhibitor.

The experimental apparatus for the suppression trials is illustrated in Fig. 12 . First, the rationality of using this experimental device to characterize the flame suppression ability of the compound is discussed here. Some related researchers use solid fuel combustion methods to evaluate flame retardant efficiency. For example, Koshiba et al. [9] compared the flame-retardant properties of calcium compounds by spreading the cellulose bed to the lower fire. Feilchenfeld et al. [41] evaluated the flame spread rate of a halide-impregnated solid fuel bed (polyester). McCater [31] studied the flame retardancy of undyed woolen cloth with pure cellulose and measured the downward flame spread rate when 184 kinds of inorganic compounds were added and not added. In addition, Izumikawa et al. [30] pointed out in the study that if the flame retardant effects the pyrolysis or degradation reaction of solid fuel, the efficiency of the flame retardant adsorption on the solid fuel is different from the efficiency of fuel combustion. However, the following content will explain that the two compounds MIL-53 and Fe-PMC do not affect the degradation reaction of cellulose, which helps to verify the rationality and accuracy of the test.

The experiment was carried out in a closed cylindrical combustion chamber with a diameter of 50 ​cm, a height of 40 ​cm, and a volume of 78.54 ​L. The pre-treated paper is sandwiched between two vertical aluminum plates (each plate: 250 ​× ​50 ​× ​1 ​mm). The ignition wire is Kanthal A-1 24 gauge resistance wire, and the current to the igniter is controlled at 6 A. The wires are formed in a zigzag pattern and placed on top of the paper to provide uniform ignition. Once the ignition is successful, the current will stop. To eliminate the influence of unstable combustion at the top of the paper sheet, the downward flame propagation rate was calculated by measuring the propagation time of the flame edge from a point 10 ​mm below the top of the sample to a point 140 ​mm below the top. The normalized downward flame spread rate is calculated by Eq (3):
(3)
V k
=
U k
U
c e l
,
where
U k
is the flame spread rate of the sample adsorbed by the flame retardant
k
. And
U
c e l
is the flame spread rate of a pure paper sheet.

The flame suppression experiment process of pure cellulose and cellulose-containing prepared flame retardants is demonstrated in Fig. 13 . As shown in Fig. 13(a), the burning flame of pure cellulose is bright, glowing, heating, flickering and rising, and the flame structure is full, mainly light yellow, indicating that cellulose is an excellent solid fuel. The flame structure of cellulose-containing MIL-53 is significantly atrophied in Fig. 13(b). From the side view, it is obvious that most of the outer area of the flame is dark blue. This is because the suppressed flame has insufficient oxygen supply, incomplete combustion, and low temperature [42]. This illustrates that the iron contained in MIL-53 participates in the combustion reaction and captures the free radicals required for combustion. As shown in Fig. 13(c), the flame spread time of cellulose-containing Fe-PMC is significantly longer than that of pure cellulose, its flame height is the lowest, and the color is the least bright. The color of the flame is dark red from the front view and dark blue from the side view. To compare the efficiency of the two iron-containing inhibitors, commercial phospho-containing flame inhibitor ADP was introduced into the experiment. The flame shape of cellulose-containing ADP was similar to MIL-53 when burning as shown in Fig. 13(d). Judging from the flame brightness, the inhibition efficiency of ADP was lower than that of Fe-PMC.

The essence of the flame is the phenomenon that air molecules around the reaction zone are heated and move at a high speed in the exothermic reaction, thereby emitting light. The heat energy released by the flame accumulates around the reaction zone, heating the surrounding air, and causing the surrounding air molecules to move at a high speed [43]. The faster the movement of air molecules will increase the temperature and brightness of the flame. The cellulose-containing Fe-PMC has the lowest flame brightness and the lowest temperature during combustion (as can be seen from Fig. 15(c)). It can be judged that the spectrum is concentrated in the infrared region, which is due to the low particle movement speed. It shows that the iron and phosphorous elements in Fe-PMC act synergistically in the combustion reaction, and reduce the flame speed by capturing combustion-free radicals to achieve the effect of suppressing the flame. For the uninhibited pure cellulose flame, the particles move the fastest during combustion, the spectrum is concentrated in the ultraviolet region, the brightness is the highest, and the temperature is the highest (as shown in Fig. 15(a)).

Fig. 14
implies the original RGB image and a corresponding binary image of flame shape for all samples. The cellulose samples added with MIL-53 and Fe-PMC can find sparks flickering above the flame during the combustion process, but this phenomenon is not found when pure cellulose samples are burned. This is because when a cellulose sample is burned, the carbon on its shallow surface reacts with oxygen to generate carbon dioxide [19], the flame retardant adsorbed on the cellulose changes from a solid-state to a fluid molten state under the action of high temperature, these molten substances are propelled by the generated carbon dioxide, leading to the phenomenon of sparks flickering [30], which shows that MIL-53 and Fe-PMC directly participate in the combustion process.

Fig. 16 illustrates normalized downward flame spreading rates as a function of MIL-53 and Fe-PMC concentrations. In this graph, the straight line is obtained by one-variable linear fitting of each data point. Since the cellulose sample and each inhibitor are prepared and burned under dry conditions, the influence of water on the downward flame propagation experiment can be ignored. As shown in Fig. 16, as the concentration increases, both V MIL-53 and V Fe-PMC gradually decrease, eventually stabilizing at 12.68 ​× ​10-4 ​mol ​g-1 and 13.69 ​× ​10-4 ​mol ​g-1, respectively. The V Fe-PMC values decrease more sharply at low concentrations and more slowly at high concentrations than V MIL-53 values. The V MIL-53 and V Fe-PMC are minimized at V MIL-53 ​= ​0.85 and V Fe-PMC ​= ​0.84 ​at C MIL-53 ​= ​13.66 ​× ​10-4 ​mol ​g-1 and C Fe-PMC ​= ​16.21 ​× ​10-4 ​mol ​g-1, respectively. It can be found that the fitting line of V Fe-PMC is located below V MIL-53, and it is more obvious in the low concentrations. It can be seen intuitively that the flame suppression efficiency of the two inhibitors is higher than that of ADP. In addition, Fe-PMC has a better flame suppression effect than MIL-53 at low concentrations, which means that the introduction of iron and phosphorus can synergistically play a better flame retardant effect.

When the flame propagates downwards, the volatile vapor is pyrolyzed in the preheating zone of the fuel to form a diffusion flame [44]. The combustible steam produced by the combustion of cellulosic fuel and the steam produced by the flame inhibitor is produced between the flame and the unburned fuel. If MIL-53 and Fe-PMC hinder the pyrolysis of cellulosic fuel and the oxidative degradation of residual carbon in the condensed state, it will affect the generation of volatile vapors of the fuel, thereby reducing the flame spread rate. If MIL-53 and Fe-PMC decompose and evaporate into the flame inhibitor in the gas phase at the flame temperature, they will be mixed with fuel vapor to reduce the flame spread rate. Therefore, it is unreasonable to judge whether the inhibitor has a solid-phase inhibitory effect or a gas-phase inhibitory effect by only relying on experimental observation methods.

Exploring the mechanism of suppression is crucial for the study of new flame inhibitors [37]. In previous studies, scholars have determined the degradation mechanism of the solid compound by analyzing TG data. For instance, Fan et al. studied the decomposition path of dry water fire extinguishing agents containing phosphorus compounds (phosphoric acid and silica) through TG analysis and reported the detailed chemical reaction kinetics mechanism for suppressing the flame of carbon-containing fuel [3]. Koshiba et al. [7] studied the detailed degradation process of solid cellulose and found that in the presence of metallocene, the decomposition temperature shifted to a higher temperature. Consequently, the analysis of TG experimental data can further explore the flame suppression mechanism of MIL-53 and Fe-PMC. In this study, the three parameters (the activation energy, the pre-exponential factor and the char yield) of the cellulose mixed with MIL-53 or Fe-PMC and pure cellulose were compared to evaluate the comprehensive inhibitory ability of the prepared inhibitors.

MIL-53 and Fe-PMC were used as prepared. Fine cellulose powder (<25 ​μm, >99.0%) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. and dried in a vacuum dryer at 105 ​°C for at least 48 ​h before use.

Dried MIL-53 and Fe-PMC were thoroughly mixed with cellulose powder in a dry glove box filled with N2. The concentration of inhibitor in the cellulose sample is shown in Table 2 . The former concentrations (items 1 and 3 in Table 1) are lower than the concentration at which V stabilizes, and the latter concentrations (items 2 and 4 in Table 1) are the concentration at which V stabilizes. To avoid the deviation of the sample concentration in the TG experiment, the preparation mass of each mixed sample is about 100 ​mg. The TG experiment was repeated 3 times for samples of the same concentration in each group, and no significant difference was observed after analyzing the data.

The pyrolysis process of cellulose has been studied clearly by predecessors [44]. Cellulose is pyrolyzed at about 300 ​°C and occurs in an anaerobic atmosphere, and residual carbon burns at >430 ​°C, which occurs in an anaerobic atmosphere. Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) and derivative thermogravimetry analysis (DTG) were performed by a TA-Q500 synchronous thermogravimetric analyzer at a temperature of 20 ​°C/min in an N2 atmosphere.

The Kissinger kinetic method [45] was used to determine the activation energy and pre-digital factors of the decomposition of cellulose samples. The Kissinger equation for the first-order reaction is:
(3a)
ln
(
β
T 2
)
=
[
ln
(
k 0
R /
E a
)
− ln
G
( a )
]
−
E a
R T
where
β
and
T
represent the heating rate and thermodynamic temperature, respectively.
k 0
and
R
respectively represent the pre-exponential factor and the gas constant (8.314 ​J ​mol-1 ​K-1).
G
( a )
is the integral form of kinetic model
f
( a )
:
(4)
a =
a
( T )
=
m 0
− m
( T )
m 0
−
m ∞
where
m 0
,
m
( T )
and
m ∞
represent the initial mass of the sample, the mass of the sample at temperature
T
and the final mass of the sample, respectively.

In order to determine the phase of inhibitors, the activation energy and pre-exponential factor of cellulose added with different amounts of MIL-53 and Fe-PMC (
E
a s
and
A s
) were compared with the activation energy and pre-exponential factor of pure cellulose (
E
a
c e l
and
A
c e l
). The inhibitors are considered to have no inhibitory effect in the solid phase if
E
a s
= E
a
c e l
and
A s
=
A
c e l
. In contrast, the inhibitors are judged to have suppression effect in the solid phase if
E
a s
> E
a
c e l
and
A s
>
A
c e l
.

The TG and DTG curves of pure cellulose, MIL-53/cellulose and Fe-PMC/cellulose are illustrated in Fig. 17 . After the water evaporates, pure cellulose begins to pyrolyze at 259 ​°C and reaches the peak pyrolysis rate at 328.90 ​°C, which is consistent with the description in the literature [44]. The char yield of pure cellulose at 780 ​°C is only 0.8%, indicating that the cellulose has been completely pyrolyzed under the action of high temperature. The char yield of cellulose-containing MIL-53 (Ck ​= ​4.19 ​× ​10-4 ​mol ​g −1) is increased to 2.338%, while the char yield of cellulose-containing Fe-PMC (Ck ​= ​4.32 ​× ​10-4 ​mol ​g −1) will be significantly increased to 8.551%. The increase in inhibitor content results in a significant increase in the char yield. The char yield of cellulose-containing MIL-53 (Ck ​= ​12.68 ​× ​10-4 ​mol ​g −1) and Fe-PMC (Ck ​= ​13.69 ​× ​10-4 ​mol ​g −1) are 5.691% and 23.197%, respectively. It can be shown that the addition of the inhibitor greatly increases the residual carbon rate of the cellulose sample, indicating that the inhibitor has a more obvious effect of inhibiting combustion in the solid phase. In addition, the inhibitory effect of Fe-PMC containing both iron and phosphorus is significantly better than MIL-53 containing only phosphorus.

The apparent activation energies, pre-exponential factors, and char yield obtained from the Kissinger method are illustrated in Table 3 . It can be seen that the apparent activation energies of pure cellulose, MIL-53/cellulose (Ck ​= ​4.19 ​× ​10-4 ​mol ​g −1), and Fe-PMC (Ck ​= ​4.32 ​× ​10-4 ​mol ​g −1) are 150.5, 157.3, and 186.7 ​kJ/mol, respectively. The logarithms of the pre-exponential factors of the pure cellulose, MIL-53/cellulose, and Fe-PMC are 11.46, 14.09, and 15.37, respectively. The apparent activation energies and pre-exponential factors of pure cellulose calculated by this method are very close to that reported in the literature [36]. These data indicate that the two prepared MC inhibitors mainly produce flame suppression effects in the solid phase rather than the gas phase. In addition, Fe-PMC significantly improves the activation energy, pre-index factor and carbon yield of cellulose samples in the solid-phase conditions, and have obvious fire suppression effects.

The main gas-phase product of MIL-53 is CO2 [22]. CO2 is a non-toxic and non-combustible gas, which can dilute the concentration of fuel and combustion-supporting agents in the combustion area and inhibit the spread of flames. The solid-phase degradation product of MIL-53 is ferroferric oxide [22]. The metal oxide produced by the degradation has a higher specific heat capacity and can absorb more external heat. At the same time, the oxide particles produced by degradation can also hinder the transmission of heat insulation. Previous studies have shown that transition metal compounds can effectively improve the thermal stability of carbon residue through catalysis [33]. The metal part and its degradation products in the MC structure can catalyze the formation of carbon from the matrix during the pyrolysis of the cellulose sample, and block the transfer of heat insulation to the matrix, thereby increasing the char yield and inhibiting the combustion process. The phosphonic acid groups and metal elements contained in Fe-PMC can promote the significant increase of carbon formation, and the presence of phosphorus elements can promote the generation of a large number of stable char [26], and then form a protective layer on the surface of the matrix, heat insulation and oxygen isolation, and prevent the further combustion of cellulose samples. In addition, as MC materials, the pyrolysis of Fe-PMC will produce metal oxide particles with porous structure, which can well inhibit fuel release and heat transfer, adsorb degradation products into the pores, and catalyze the formation of carbon, to achieve the effect of flame suppression.

To develop a fire extinguishing agent with better fire extinguishing performance than a phosphorus-containing extinguishing agent, the MIL-53 and Fe-PMC were prepared and their combustion inhibition efficiencies were investigated. According to the results given in this article, the following key findings can be summarized below.
1.
FT-IR and XRD results confirmed that MIL-53 and Fe-PMC were synthesized by bridging the ligands of terephthalic acid and phytic acid with iron ions as metal cations. SEM confirmed that the two prepared materials had the distinct morphological characteristics of MC, and both were reticulated porous materials formed by the accumulation and connection of small particles. Compared with MIL-53, the particle size distribution of polyhedral Fe-PMC is more uniform (1∼2 ​μm), and the pore network structure on its surface grows closely. XPS data after peak fitting confirmed that Fe-PMC not only contained 6.24% iron but also introduced 13.64% phosphorus due to the coordination with phytic acid.
2.
The results of the combustion inhibition test showed that MIL-53 and Fe-PMC showed an increasing inhibition effect in the tested concentration range. However, in the low concentration region, the inhibition efficiency of Fe-PMC containing both iron and phosphorus was significantly higher than that of MIL-53 containing only iron.
3.
TG results confirmed that MIL-53 and Fe-PMC mainly produced combustion inhibition in the solid phase rather than in the gas phase. Compared with MIL-53, the introduction of Fe-PMC significantly increased the char yield, apparent activation energy and pre-exponential factor of cellulose sample pyrolysis. The higher content of char serves as a protective layer for heat insulation and oxygen isolation, which mainly explains the difference in the inhibition ability between MIL-53 and Fe-PMC.

In this study, phytic acid was used as an organic ligand to prepare MC with iron ions. In addition, its combustion inhibition efficiency and mechanism were verified through experiments. These findings will help in the development of a new type of flame inhibitor with little or no phosphorus, and better performance.

Haoran Jiang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yong Hu: Visualization. Yong Jiang: Data curation, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science of China (No.51576183), the National Key R&D Program of China (No.2016YFC0801505), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (No.WK2320000048 and WK2320000054).
